1:
"Children, obey your parents in the Lord: for this is right." The witnesses note the duty of children to obey their parents. Chrysostom notes that, as one forming a body places the head first, then the neck, then the feet, so Paul proceeds by gradual advances — having spoken of the husband and the wife, he comes now to the third rank, that of children, over whom both parents have authority. The witnesses note Paul charging children to obey their parents in the Lord, for this is right; the children's obedience as a thing right in itself — that Paul lays on children the duty of obedience to their parents, "in the Lord" (in all things agreeable to the Lord's will, and as part of their obedience to him), grounding the command not merely on parental authority but on the plain rightness of the thing: that children should obey those who gave them life is just and fitting in the very order of nature and of God; obey your parents in the Lord. The witnesses note children, obey your parents in the Lord. The witnesses note for this is right.

The witnesses note the duty of children to obey their parents in the Lord — a thing right in itself, just and fitting in the order of nature and of God.

So the witnesses make the verse the children's duty of obedience. The lesson the expositors draw is that obedience to parents is right in itself, grounded in the order of nature and of God. The witnesses note the command: "children, obey your parents" — Paul charges children to obey their parents; the command; the obey; the children; the parents; the duty. The expositors observe the in the Lord: "in the Lord" — obey in the Lord (in all agreeable to his will, as part of obedience to him); the in the Lord; the his will; the agreeable; the as to him; the bounded. The witnesses press the rightness: "for this is right" — this obedience is right (just, fitting); the rightness; the right; the just; the fitting; the proper. The expositors draw the lesson on the order of nature: that children should obey those who gave them life is just in the order of nature and of God; the order of nature; the gave them life; the just; the of God; the fitting. The witnesses observe Chrysostom's note: Chrysostom notes Paul proceeds by gradual advances — husband, wife, now children; the gradual advances; the husband; the wife; the children; the third rank. The expositors note the parental authority: both parents have authority over the children; the parental authority; the both parents; the over the children; the authority; the rank. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the children obey your parents in the Lord, the for this is right, and the duty of obedience. The verse thus records the children's duty of obedience — teaching that obedience to parents is right in itself, grounded in the order of nature and of God.
2:
"Honour thy father and mother; (which is the first commandment with promise;)" The witnesses note the command to honour parents, the first with a promise. Gill notes that parents are to be honoured in thought, word, and gesture — highly thought of, spoken to and of with reverence, their corrections submitted to, their infirmities covered, more than merely loved and feared. The witnesses note Paul citing the command to honour father and mother, noting it is the first commandment with a promise attached; the honour due to parents, sealed by a promise — that Paul grounds the children's obedience in the fifth commandment of the Decalogue, which requires not bare obedience but honour (reverence, esteem, submission, and care) toward both parents, and which he marks as "the first commandment with promise," the first of the precepts to which a particular reward is annexed, so that God himself has crowned the honouring of parents with a special blessing; the first commandment with promise. The witnesses note honour thy father and mother. The witnesses note which is the first commandment with promise.

The witnesses note the command to honour father and mother — the first commandment with a promise attached, the honouring of parents crowned with a special blessing.

So the witnesses make the verse the honour due to parents, sealed by a promise. The lesson the expositors draw is that the fifth commandment requires honour toward parents and is crowned by God with a special promise. The witnesses note the honour: "honour thy father and mother" — Gill notes parents are to be honoured in thought, word, and gesture; the honour; the father and mother; the reverence; the esteem; the submission. The expositors observe the more than obedience: the honour is more than bare obedience — reverence, esteem, care; the more than obedience; the reverence; the esteem; the care; the honour. The witnesses press the first with promise: "which is the first commandment with promise" — it is the first commandment with a promise attached; the first with promise; the the fifth; the the Decalogue; the promise; the annexed. The expositors draw the lesson on the special reward: it is the first of the precepts to which a particular reward is annexed; the special reward; the the first; the particular reward; the annexed; the promise. The witnesses observe the crowned by God: God himself has crowned the honouring of parents with a special blessing; the crowned by God; the special blessing; the the honouring; the promise; the reward. The expositors note the fifth commandment: the command is the fifth of the Decalogue; the fifth commandment; the the Decalogue; the the fifth; the the law; the precept. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the honour thy father and mother, the which is the first commandment with promise, and the honour sealed by a promise. The verse thus records the honour due to parents, sealed by a promise — teaching that the fifth commandment requires honour toward parents and is crowned by God with a special promise.
3:
"That it may be well with thee, and thou mayest live long on the earth." The witnesses note the promise annexed to honouring parents. Gill notes the promise is that it may be well with the child, in this world and that which is to come. Barnes notes the whole commandment in Deuteronomy adds "that thy days may be prolonged" in the land which the Lord gives. The witnesses note Paul stating the promise — that it may be well with the child and he may live long on the earth; the promise of well-being and long life — that Paul gives the very promise attached to the fifth commandment: that the child who honours his parents shall fare well and enjoy length of days, a promise which holds generally (the honouring of parents tending, in God's ordinary providence, to a prosperous and lengthened life), and which the gospel raises to its higher fulfilment in the well-being and eternal life of the obedient; that it may be well with thee. The witnesses note that it may be well with thee. The witnesses note thou mayest live long on the earth.

The witnesses note the promise — that it may be well with the child and he live long — the honouring of parents tending, in God's providence, to a prosperous and lengthened life.

So the witnesses make the verse the promise annexed to honouring parents. The lesson the expositors draw is the promise of well-being and long life to the child who honours his parents. The witnesses note the well: "that it may be well with thee" — Gill notes the promise that it may be well with the child; the well; the with thee; the fare well; the prosperous; the blessing. The expositors observe the long life: "thou mayest live long on the earth" — and that he may live long on the earth; the long life; the live long; the length of days; the on the earth; the prolonged. The witnesses press the promise: the promise is attached to the fifth commandment; the promise; the attached; the the commandment; the the fifth; the annexed. The expositors draw the lesson on the general holding: the promise holds generally — honouring parents tends to a prosperous and lengthened life; the general holding; the tends; the prosperous; the lengthened; the providence. The witnesses observe the ordinary providence: in God's ordinary providence, the honouring of parents brings well-being; the ordinary providence; the honouring; the brings; the well-being; the providence. The expositors note the higher fulfilment: the gospel raises it to its higher fulfilment in eternal life; the higher fulfilment; the the gospel; the eternal life; the raised; the obedient. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the that it may be well with thee, the thou mayest live long on the earth, and the promise. The verse thus records the promise annexed to honouring parents — teaching the promise of well-being and long life to the child who honours his parents.
4:
"And, ye fathers, provoke not your children to wrath: but bring them up in the nurture and admonition of the Lord." The witnesses note the parents' duty toward their children. Gill notes fathers provoke their children to wrath by unjust and unreasonable commands, reproachful language, frequent public chidings, passionate expressions, partiality, denying necessaries, severe blows, and inhuman usage. The witnesses note Paul charging fathers not to provoke their children to wrath, but to bring them up in the nurture and admonition of the Lord; the balance of parental authority with loving discipline — that Paul, having required children's obedience, fences the parents' authority against abuse: they are not to exasperate their children by harshness, injustice, or unreasonable severity (which provokes resentment and discouragement), but to rear them with godly discipline ("nurture") and instruction ("admonition") in the Lord's ways, training them up for God; bring them up in the nurture and admonition of the Lord. The witnesses note provoke not your children to wrath. The witnesses note bring them up in the nurture and admonition of the Lord.

The witnesses note the parents' duty — not to provoke children to wrath by harshness, but to bring them up in the godly nurture and admonition of the Lord.

So the witnesses make the verse the parents' duty toward their children. The lesson the expositors draw is the balance of authority with loving discipline, rearing children in the Lord's ways without exasperating them. The witnesses note the provoke not: "provoke not your children to wrath" — Gill notes fathers provoke by harshness, injustice, severity; the provoke not; the to wrath; the harshness; the injustice; the severity. The expositors observe the abuse fenced: the parents' authority is fenced against abuse — not to exasperate; the abuse fenced; the exasperate; the resentment; the discouragement; the harshness. The witnesses press the bring up: "bring them up in the nurture and admonition of the Lord" — but rear them in godly discipline and instruction; the bring up; the nurture; the admonition; the discipline; the instruction. The expositors draw the lesson on the godly rearing: parents are to rear children with godly discipline and instruction in the Lord's ways; the godly rearing; the discipline; the instruction; the the Lord's ways; the train. The witnesses observe the not exasperating: they are not to provoke resentment by unreasonable severity; the not exasperating; the resentment; the unreasonable; the severity; the harshness. The expositors note the training for God: the children are to be trained up for God; the training for God; the trained; the for God; the the Lord; the reared. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the provoke not your children to wrath, the bring them up in the nurture and admonition of the Lord, and the parents' duty. The verse thus records the parents' duty toward their children — teaching the balance of authority with loving discipline, rearing children in the Lord's ways without exasperating them.
5:
"Servants, be obedient to them that are your masters according to the flesh, with fear and trembling, in singleness of your heart, as unto Christ;" The witnesses note the duty of servants to obey their masters. Chrysostom notes that not husband only, nor wife, nor children, but virtuous servants also contribute to the organization of a house; Paul comes to them last, as last in dignity and rank, yet addresses them in an advanced tone. The witnesses note Paul charging servants to obey their earthly masters with fear and trembling, in singleness of heart, as unto Christ; the service rendered to masters as service to Christ — that Paul lays on servants the duty of hearty obedience to their masters "according to the flesh" (their earthly masters, not their lords in spiritual things), and that with reverent care (fear and trembling), sincere and undivided purpose (singleness of heart), and above all "as unto Christ," so that the lowliest service, done as to the Lord himself, is dignified and made an act of worship; as unto Christ. The witnesses note servants, be obedient to your masters according to the flesh. The witnesses note with fear and trembling, in singleness of your heart, as unto Christ.

The witnesses note the duty of servants to obey their earthly masters with fear and trembling, in singleness of heart, "as unto Christ" — the lowliest service made worship.

So the witnesses make the verse the duty of servants rendered as to Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is that the servant's hearty, sincere obedience, done as to Christ, dignifies the lowliest service. The witnesses note the obedience: "servants, be obedient to them that are your masters according to the flesh" — Paul charges servants to obey their earthly masters; the obedience; the servants; the masters; the according to the flesh; the earthly. The expositors observe the fear and trembling: "with fear and trembling" — with reverent care (fear and trembling); the fear and trembling; the reverent care; the diligence; the conscientious; the careful. The witnesses press the singleness: "in singleness of your heart" — with sincere, undivided purpose (singleness of heart); the singleness; the sincere; the undivided; the heart; the genuine. The expositors draw the lesson on the as unto Christ: "as unto Christ" — and above all as unto Christ; the as unto Christ; the to the Lord; the worship; the dignified; the service. The witnesses observe the lowliest service dignified: the lowliest service, done as to the Lord himself, is dignified; the lowliest service; the dignified; the as to the Lord; the worship; the made noble. The expositors note Chrysostom's note: Chrysostom notes servants too contribute to the house, addressed in an advanced tone; the contribute; the the house; the advanced tone; the last in rank; the addressed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the servants be obedient to your masters according to the flesh, the as unto Christ, and the service as to Christ. The verse thus records the duty of servants rendered as to Christ — teaching that the servant's hearty, sincere obedience, done as to Christ, dignifies the lowliest service.
6:
"Not with eyeservice, as menpleasers; but as the servants of Christ, doing the will of God from the heart;" The witnesses note the warning against eyeservice and the call to heart-service. Gill notes eyeservice is doing work only when under the master's eye, then pretending great diligence to ingratiate oneself, but neglecting his business when he is absent; whereas they ought to serve in his absence as in his presence. The witnesses note Paul charging servants to serve not with eyeservice as menpleasers, but as servants of Christ, doing the will of God from the heart; the sincere service that works for God, not appearance — that Paul forbids the false, eye-pleasing service that labours only when watched and only to win favour, and requires instead a true, inward service: working as Christ's own servants (whose eye is always on them), doing the will of God from the heart, with the same faithfulness whether the earthly master sees or not; not with eyeservice, as menpleasers. The witnesses note not with eyeservice, as menpleasers. The witnesses note as the servants of Christ, doing the will of God from the heart.

The witnesses note the warning against eyeservice — serving not to win favour when watched, but as Christ's servants, doing God's will from the heart.

So the witnesses make the verse the sincere service that works for God. The lesson the expositors draw is the call to true, inward service, faithful whether the master sees or not, as Christ's own servant. The witnesses note the eyeservice: "not with eyeservice, as menpleasers" — Gill notes eyeservice is working only when watched, to win favour; the eyeservice; the menpleasers; the only when watched; the win favour; the false. The expositors observe the neglect when absent: the eye-servant neglects the master's business when he is absent; the neglect; the when absent; the the business; the neglected; the false. The witnesses press the servants of Christ: "but as the servants of Christ" — but as Christ's own servants; the servants of Christ; the Christ's own; the his servants; the his eye; the true. The expositors draw the lesson on the will of God from the heart: "doing the will of God from the heart" — doing the will of God from the heart; the will of God; the from the heart; the inward; the sincere; the true. The witnesses observe the faithful whether seen or not: the true servant works the same whether seen or not; the faithful; the whether seen or not; the same; the always; the sincere. The expositors note the Christ's eye always on them: Christ's eye is always on his servants; the Christ's eye; the always; the on them; the watching; the true. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the not with eyeservice as menpleasers, the as the servants of Christ doing the will of God from the heart, and the sincere service. The verse thus records the sincere service that works for God — teaching the call to true, inward service, faithful whether the master sees or not, as Christ's own servant.
7:
"With good will doing service, as to the Lord, and not to men;" The witnesses note the call to cheerful, willing service rendered to the Lord. Gill notes that service is to be done with good will — not grudgingly, with an ill will, nor only when forced, but with a ready mind and cheerful spirit, taking delight in the work and reckoning it a pleasure to serve. The witnesses note Paul charging servants to do service with good will, as to the Lord and not to men; the willing, glad service offered ultimately to Christ — that Paul calls for service rendered not reluctantly or as a hated necessity, but with a glad and ready heart, and lifts the whole transaction above the earthly relation: the servant is to regard his work as done "to the Lord, and not to men," so that even bond-service becomes a willing offering to Christ, who is the true Master being served; with good will doing service, as to the Lord. The witnesses note with good will doing service. The witnesses note as to the Lord, and not to men.

The witnesses note the call to cheerful, willing service "as to the Lord, and not to men" — even bond-service made a glad offering to Christ, the true Master served.

So the witnesses make the verse the willing service rendered to the Lord. The lesson the expositors draw is that service is to be done gladly, as to Christ, the true Master, not reluctantly to men. The witnesses note the good will: "with good will doing service" — Gill notes service with good will, not grudgingly, but with a ready, cheerful spirit; the good will; the not grudgingly; the ready; the cheerful; the glad. The expositors observe the not forced: the service is not only when forced, but willing; the not forced; the willing; the ready mind; the cheerful; the glad. The witnesses press the to the Lord: "as to the Lord, and not to men" — as to the Lord, and not to men; the to the Lord; the not to men; the to Christ; the the true Master; the lifted. The expositors draw the lesson on the offering to Christ: the servant regards his work as done to the Lord, so even bond-service is a willing offering to Christ; the offering to Christ; the to the Lord; the bond-service; the willing offering; the lifted. The witnesses observe the glad heart: the work is to be done with a glad and ready heart; the glad heart; the ready; the willing; the cheerful; the glad. The expositors note the true Master served: Christ is the true Master being served; the true Master; the Christ; the served; the the Lord; the ultimate. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the with good will doing service, the as to the Lord and not to men, and the willing service. The verse thus records the willing service rendered to the Lord — teaching that service is to be done gladly, as to Christ, the true Master, not reluctantly to men.
8:
"Knowing that whatsoever good thing any man doeth, the same shall he receive of the Lord, whether he be bond or free." The witnesses note the assurance of reward for good done, regardless of station. Gill notes the good thing is done according to the will of God, from right principles in the heart, with a view to God's glory. Barnes notes that whatever a man does that is right, he shall be rewarded, no matter his rank in life. The witnesses note Paul assuring servants that whatever good thing any man does, the same he shall receive of the Lord, whether bond or free; the impartial reward of the Lord for faithful service — that Paul crowns his charge to servants with the cheering certainty that the Lord, the great Master, marks and rewards every good deed, and that without respect to a man's earthly station, so that the lowliest bond-servant, faithful in his work, shall receive of Christ the same reward as the free, his humble service not overlooked but recompensed; whatsoever good thing any man doeth. The witnesses note whatsoever good thing any man doeth, the same shall he receive of the Lord. The witnesses note whether he be bond or free.

The witnesses note the assurance that the Lord rewards every good deed, whether bond or free — the lowliest servant's faithful work not overlooked but recompensed.

So the witnesses make the verse the impartial reward of the Lord. The lesson the expositors draw is that the Lord rewards every good deed without respect to a man's earthly station. The witnesses note the good thing: "whatsoever good thing any man doeth" — Gill notes the good done according to God's will, from right principles; the good thing; the according to God's will; the right principles; the for God's glory; the done. The expositors observe the reward: "the same shall he receive of the Lord" — the same he shall receive of the Lord; the reward; the receive; the of the Lord; the recompensed; the marked. The witnesses press the bond or free: "whether he be bond or free" — whether bond or free; the bond or free; the regardless; the station; the impartial; the both. The expositors draw the lesson on the impartial reward: the Lord rewards every good deed without respect to station; the impartial reward; the every good deed; the without respect; the station; the rewards. The witnesses observe the lowliest not overlooked: the lowliest bond-servant's faithful work is not overlooked but recompensed; the lowliest; the not overlooked; the recompensed; the faithful; the rewarded. The expositors note the same reward: the bond-servant shall receive the same reward as the free; the same reward; the bond and free; the same; the equal; the recompensed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the whatsoever good thing any man doeth the same shall he receive of the Lord, the whether he be bond or free, and the impartial reward. The verse thus records the impartial reward of the Lord — teaching that the Lord rewards every good deed without respect to a man's earthly station.
9:
"And, ye masters, do the same things unto them, forbearing threatening: knowing that your Master also is in heaven; neither is there respect of persons with him." The witnesses note the masters' duty toward their servants. Gill notes "do the same things" refers not to service and obedience, but to singleness of heart, benevolence, humanity, and a regard to Christ and the will of God, doing their duty as they would have their servants do theirs. The witnesses note Paul charging masters to do the same things to their servants, forbearing threatening, knowing their own Master is in heaven, with whom is no respect of persons; the masters' equity grounded in their own accountability to God — that Paul lays on masters too a duty: to treat their servants with the same conscientious goodwill, sparing harsh threats and cruelty, and to remember that they themselves have a Master in heaven before whom master and servant stand alike, for God shows no respect of persons, judging the great and the lowly by the same impartial standard; your Master also is in heaven. The witnesses note ye masters, do the same things unto them, forbearing threatening. The witnesses note neither is there respect of persons with him.

The witnesses note the masters' duty — to treat servants with goodwill, forbearing threats, remembering their own Master in heaven, with whom is no respect of persons.

So the witnesses make the verse the masters' equity grounded in their accountability to God. The lesson the expositors draw is that masters owe their servants conscientious goodwill, mindful of their own Master in heaven who shows no partiality. The witnesses note the same things: "ye masters, do the same things unto them" — Gill notes masters owe singleness of heart, benevolence, goodwill; the same things; the singleness of heart; the benevolence; the goodwill; the duty. The expositors observe the forbearing threatening: "forbearing threatening" — sparing harsh threats and cruelty; the forbearing threatening; the harsh threats; the cruelty; the spared; the gentle. The witnesses press the Master in heaven: "knowing that your Master also is in heaven" — they have a Master in heaven; the Master in heaven; the their own Master; the accountability; the above; the God. The expositors draw the lesson on the accountability: masters are accountable to their own Master in heaven; the accountability; the their Master; the in heaven; the answer; the judged. The witnesses observe the no respect of persons: "neither is there respect of persons with him" — God shows no respect of persons; the no respect of persons; the impartial; the master and servant alike; the same standard; the judged. The expositors note the same standard: God judges the great and the lowly by the same impartial standard; the same standard; the great and lowly; the impartial; the judged; the equal. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the ye masters do the same things unto them forbearing threatening, the neither is there respect of persons with him, and the masters' equity. The verse thus records the masters' equity grounded in accountability to God — teaching that masters owe their servants conscientious goodwill, mindful of their own Master in heaven who shows no partiality.
10:
"Finally, my brethren, be strong in the Lord, and in the power of his might." The witnesses note the call to be strong in the Lord's might. Gill notes Paul addresses the saints as his brethren, not merely as a familiar way of speaking, but as regenerate persons of the same household of God, in humility and affection. The witnesses note Paul, concluding his exhortations, calling the brethren to be strong in the Lord and in the power of his might; the call to draw strength from the Lord alone — that Paul, as he turns to the great theme of spiritual warfare, sounds the keynote: the believer is not to trust his own strength (which is no match for his enemies), but to be strong "in the Lord, and in the power of his might," drawing all his courage and ability for the battle from union with Christ and the inexhaustible store of God's own mighty power; be strong in the Lord, and in the power of his might. The witnesses note be strong in the Lord. The witnesses note in the power of his might.

The witnesses note the call to be strong in the Lord and in the power of his might — the believer drawing all his strength for the battle from God, not himself.

So the witnesses make the verse the call to strength in the Lord. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer is to draw all his strength for the warfare from the Lord, not himself. The witnesses note the brethren: "finally, my brethren" — Gill notes Paul addresses the saints as brethren, in affection; the brethren; the saints; the affection; the regenerate; the household. The expositors observe the be strong: "be strong in the Lord" — be strong in the Lord; the be strong; the in the Lord; the strength; the courage; the union. The witnesses press the power of his might: "and in the power of his might" — and in the power of his might; the power of his might; the God's power; the mighty; the inexhaustible; the store. The expositors draw the lesson on not his own strength: the believer is not to trust his own strength, no match for his enemies; the not his own; the not trust; the no match; the enemies; the weak. The witnesses observe the from union with Christ: the believer draws his courage and ability from union with Christ; the from union; the with Christ; the courage; the ability; the drawn. The expositors note the keynote of warfare: this sounds the keynote as Paul turns to spiritual warfare; the keynote; the warfare; the turns; the the battle; the introduced. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the be strong in the Lord, the in the power of his might, and the strength from the Lord. The verse thus records the call to strength in the Lord — teaching that the believer is to draw all his strength for the warfare from the Lord, not himself.
11:
"Put on the whole armour of God, that ye may be able to stand against the wiles of the devil." The witnesses note the command to put on the whole armour of God. Gill notes the armour is not what God himself wears, but what he has provided and furnishes his people with; it is called "the armour of God" because prepared and bestowed by him, and because it is divine and spiritual, not carnal. The witnesses note Paul commanding them to put on the whole armour of God, that they may stand against the wiles of the devil; the full divine armour against the devil's craft — that Paul calls the believer to be clad not in part but in the whole (complete) armour that God supplies, every piece being needful, so that, fully equipped with God's own provision, he may be able to stand his ground against the devil — not his open force only, but his "wiles," the cunning, crafty stratagems by which the enemy seeks to deceive and overthrow; the whole armour of God... against the wiles of the devil. The witnesses note put on the whole armour of God. The witnesses note that ye may be able to stand against the wiles of the devil.

The witnesses note the command to put on the whole armour of God — the complete divine armour, that the believer may stand against the devil's crafty wiles.

So the witnesses make the verse the command to put on the whole armour. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer needs the complete divine armour to stand against the devil's cunning stratagems. The witnesses note the whole armour: "put on the whole armour of God" — Gill notes the armour God provides, divine and spiritual; the whole armour; the of God; the provided; the divine; the spiritual. The expositors observe the complete: the believer is to be clad in the whole (complete) armour, every piece needful; the complete; the whole; the every piece; the needful; the full. The witnesses press the able to stand: "that ye may be able to stand" — that he may stand his ground; the able to stand; the stand; the his ground; the hold; the firm. The expositors draw the lesson on the wiles: "against the wiles of the devil" — against the devil's wiles (cunning stratagems); the wiles; the cunning; the stratagems; the craft; the deceive. The witnesses observe the not open force only: the believer faces not the devil's open force only, but his crafty wiles; the not open force; the wiles; the craft; the deceive; the overthrow. The expositors note the God's provision: the armour is God's own provision, fully equipping the believer; the God's provision; the fully equipping; the supplied; the divine; the armour. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the put on the whole armour of God, the that ye may be able to stand against the wiles of the devil, and the full divine armour. The verse thus records the command to put on the whole armour — teaching that the believer needs the complete divine armour to stand against the devil's cunning stratagems.
12:
"For we wrestle not against flesh and blood, but against principalities, against powers, against the rulers of the darkness of this world, against spiritual wickedness in high places." The witnesses note the true nature of the believer's spiritual enemies. Gill notes this is a reason for the saints to be strong and put on the whole armour, since their enemies are not "flesh and blood" — frail mortal men, such as were wrestled against in the games — but spiritual foes. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the believer's wrestling is not against flesh and blood, but against principalities, powers, the rulers of this world's darkness, and spiritual wickedness in high places; the foe a host of mighty evil spirits — that Paul reveals why such complete armour is needed: the real enemies are not human (mere mortal men), but a vast and organized army of evil spirits — the fallen angels in their ranks and orders (principalities, powers), the dark rulers of this present age, hosts of spiritual wickedness in the heavenly places, mighty, malignant, and unseen, against whom no fleshly strength can prevail; we wrestle not against flesh and blood. The witnesses note we wrestle not against flesh and blood. The witnesses note against principalities, against powers... spiritual wickedness in high places.

The witnesses note the true enemies — not flesh and blood, but a host of mighty evil spirits, principalities and powers, against whom no fleshly strength can prevail.

So the witnesses make the verse the nature of the spiritual enemies. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer's foes are a vast army of evil spirits, requiring the divine armour. The witnesses note the not flesh and blood: "we wrestle not against flesh and blood" — Gill notes the enemies are not frail mortal men; the not flesh and blood; the not mortal men; the not human; the spiritual; the foes. The expositors observe the principalities and powers: "against principalities, against powers" — the fallen angels in their ranks (principalities, powers); the principalities; the powers; the fallen angels; the ranks; the orders. The witnesses press the rulers of darkness: "against the rulers of the darkness of this world" — the dark rulers of this present age; the rulers of darkness; the this world; the present age; the dark; the powers. The expositors draw the lesson on the spiritual wickedness: "against spiritual wickedness in high places" — hosts of spiritual wickedness in the heavenly places; the spiritual wickedness; the high places; the heavenly places; the hosts; the evil. The witnesses observe the mighty and unseen: the enemies are mighty, malignant, and unseen; the mighty; the malignant; the unseen; the powerful; the evil spirits. The expositors note the no fleshly strength: against such foes no fleshly strength can prevail — hence the divine armour; the no fleshly strength; the cannot prevail; the the divine armour; the needed; the foes. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the we wrestle not against flesh and blood, the against principalities against powers, and the spiritual enemies. The verse thus records the nature of the spiritual enemies — teaching that the believer's foes are a vast army of evil spirits, requiring the divine armour.
13:
"Wherefore take unto you the whole armour of God, that ye may be able to withstand in the evil day, and having done all, to stand." The witnesses note the renewed command to take the whole armour. Gill notes this is a repetition of the exhortation, made necessary by the powerful enemies just mentioned, and serves to explain what is meant by putting on the armour, leading to the account of its several parts. Barnes notes "the evil day" is the day of temptation and trial. The witnesses note Paul repeating the command to take the whole armour of God, that they may withstand in the evil day and, having done all, stand; the full equipment for the day of trial and final standing — that Paul, in view of such mighty foes, urges again the taking up of God's complete armour, with a double aim: that the believer may "withstand" (resist and hold out) in the evil day (the season of fierce temptation and assault), and that, when the whole conflict is over and all is done, he may still be found standing, unfallen and victorious; take unto you the whole armour of God. The witnesses note take unto you the whole armour of God. The witnesses note to withstand in the evil day, and having done all, to stand.

The witnesses note the renewed command to take the whole armour — to withstand in the evil day and, having done all, to stand unfallen and victorious.

So the witnesses make the verse the full equipment for the day of trial. The lesson the expositors draw is that the believer takes the whole armour to resist in the evil day and stand victorious at the last. The witnesses note the taking: "take unto you the whole armour of God" — Gill notes the repeated exhortation to take the armour; the taking; the whole armour; the of God; the repeated; the command. The expositors observe the withstand: "that ye may be able to withstand in the evil day" — to withstand (resist, hold out) in the evil day; the withstand; the resist; the hold out; the the evil day; the trial. The witnesses press the evil day: Barnes notes the evil day is the day of temptation and trial; the evil day; the temptation; the trial; the assault; the fierce. The expositors draw the lesson on the having done all: "and having done all, to stand" — having done all, to stand (found standing at the last); the having done all; the to stand; the at the last; the unfallen; the victorious. The witnesses observe the double aim: the double aim is to resist in the day, and to stand when all is done; the double aim; the resist; the stand; the the day; the at the end. The expositors note the unfallen and victorious: when the conflict is over, the believer is found standing, unfallen; the unfallen; the victorious; the standing; the conflict over; the won. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the take unto you the whole armour of God, the to withstand in the evil day and having done all to stand, and the full equipment. The verse thus records the full equipment for the day of trial — teaching that the believer takes the whole armour to resist in the evil day and stand victorious at the last.
14:
"Stand therefore, having your loins girt about with truth, and having on the breastplate of righteousness;" The witnesses note the first pieces of armour — the girdle of truth and the breastplate of righteousness. Chrysostom notes that, having drawn up this army and roused their zeal and inspired them with courage, Paul now proceeds to arm them — for arms would have been of no use had they not first been posted each in his place and their spirit aroused. The witnesses note Paul calling them to stand, with loins girt with truth and the breastplate of righteousness on; the girdle of truth and the breastplate of righteousness — that Paul begins to name the pieces: the believer's loins are to be girt with "truth" (the truth of the gospel held fast, and sincerity and integrity of heart, which braces and binds up the whole man for action, as the soldier's belt), and his heart guarded by the "breastplate of righteousness" (the righteousness of Christ for justification, and a holy life, which covers the vital part against the enemy's thrusts); the loins girt with truth, the breastplate of righteousness. The witnesses note loins girt about with truth. The witnesses note the breastplate of righteousness.

The witnesses note the first armour — the loins girt with truth and the breastplate of righteousness — bracing the man for action and guarding the heart.

So the witnesses make the verse the girdle of truth and breastplate of righteousness. The lesson the expositors draw is that truth braces the whole man for action and righteousness guards the heart against the enemy. The witnesses note the standing: "stand therefore" — the believer is to stand; the standing; the stand; the firm; the his ground; the posted. The expositors observe Chrysostom's note: Chrysostom notes Paul first posted the army, then armed them; the posted; the armed; the the army; the zeal; the courage. The witnesses press the loins girt: "having your loins girt about with truth" — the loins girt with truth (the gospel held fast, sincerity, integrity); the loins girt; the truth; the the gospel; the sincerity; the integrity. The expositors draw the lesson on the girdle: truth, like the soldier's belt, braces and binds up the whole man for action; the girdle; the the belt; the braces; the binds up; the action. The witnesses observe the breastplate: "having on the breastplate of righteousness" — the breastplate of righteousness (Christ's righteousness and a holy life); the breastplate; the righteousness; the Christ's righteousness; the holy life; the guard. The expositors note the guarding the heart: the breastplate covers the vital part (the heart) against the enemy's thrusts; the guarding the heart; the the vital part; the the heart; the the thrusts; the covered. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the loins girt about with truth, the the breastplate of righteousness, and the first armour. The verse thus records the girdle of truth and breastplate of righteousness — teaching that truth braces the whole man for action and righteousness guards the heart against the enemy.
15:
"And your feet shod with the preparation of the gospel of peace;" The witnesses note the shoes of the gospel of peace. Gill notes the gospel is called the gospel of peace because it makes men of peaceable tempers, gives peace to distressed minds, directs the way to eternal peace, and publishes the peace made by the blood of Christ. The witnesses note Paul directing that their feet be shod with the preparation of the gospel of peace; the gospel of peace as the soldier's footing — that Paul, continuing the figure, gives the believer shoes for his feet: the firm footing and readiness that the gospel of peace supplies, so that, as the soldier needs sure footing to stand and march without slipping or being wounded, the believer, established in the gospel (which proclaims peace with God through Christ's blood) and ready to walk and witness in it, has steady ground beneath him for the whole campaign; feet shod with the preparation of the gospel of peace. The witnesses note your feet shod. The witnesses note the preparation of the gospel of peace.

The witnesses note the feet shod with the gospel of peace — the firm footing and readiness the gospel supplies, steady ground for the believer's whole campaign.

So the witnesses make the verse the shoes of the gospel of peace. The lesson the expositors draw is that the gospel of peace gives the believer firm footing and readiness for the campaign. The witnesses note the shod feet: "your feet shod" — the believer's feet are shod (with shoes); the shod feet; the the feet; the shoes; the footing; the equipped. The expositors observe the gospel of peace: "the preparation of the gospel of peace" — Gill notes the gospel of peace (proclaiming peace with God); the gospel of peace; the peace with God; the Christ's blood; the peaceable; the eternal peace. The witnesses press the firm footing: the shoes give firm footing and readiness; the firm footing; the readiness; the sure footing; the steady; the stand. The expositors draw the lesson on the soldier's footing: as the soldier needs sure footing to stand and march, the believer needs steady ground; the soldier's footing; the sure footing; the march; the without slipping; the steady. The witnesses observe the established in the gospel: the believer is established in the gospel (which proclaims peace through Christ's blood); the established; the the gospel; the peace; the Christ's blood; the grounded. The expositors note the ready to walk and witness: the believer is ready to walk and witness in the gospel; the ready; the walk; the witness; the the gospel; the campaign. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the your feet shod, the the preparation of the gospel of peace, and the firm footing. The verse thus records the shoes of the gospel of peace — teaching that the gospel of peace gives the believer firm footing and readiness for the campaign.
16:
"Above all, taking the shield of faith, wherewith ye shall be able to quench all the fiery darts of the wicked." The witnesses note the shield of faith that quenches the fiery darts. Gill notes the shield of faith may be understood of the grace of faith, like a golden shield, by which mighty things are done and the enemy not only repelled but conquered; or rather of the object of faith, Christ. The witnesses note Paul calling them, above all, to take the shield of faith, by which they can quench all the fiery darts of the wicked one; faith the shield that extinguishes Satan's darts — that Paul gives faith a place above the rest, the shield that covers the whole man and is moved to meet every assault: by faith (laying hold of Christ and God's promises) the believer catches and quenches the "fiery darts" of the wicked one — the flaming arrows of temptation, doubt, blasphemy, and despair the devil shoots at the soul — so that they fall harmless and burnt out; the shield of faith... quench all the fiery darts. The witnesses note above all, taking the shield of faith. The witnesses note quench all the fiery darts of the wicked.

The witnesses note the shield of faith, above all — by which the believer quenches all the fiery darts of the wicked one, the flaming arrows of temptation and doubt.

So the witnesses make the verse the shield of faith. The lesson the expositors draw is that faith, above the rest, catches and quenches Satan's fiery darts. The witnesses note the above all: "above all, taking the shield of faith" — Gill notes faith given a place above the rest; the above all; the the shield; the faith; the chief; the placed. The expositors observe the shield: the shield of faith covers the whole man and meets every assault; the shield; the covers; the the whole man; the meets; the assault. The witnesses press the faith: faith lays hold of Christ and God's promises; the faith; the lays hold; the Christ; the the promises; the grasps. The expositors draw the lesson on quenching the darts: "wherewith ye shall be able to quench all the fiery darts of the wicked" — by faith the believer quenches the fiery darts; the quenching; the the fiery darts; the the wicked; the catches; the extinguishes. The witnesses observe the fiery darts: the fiery darts are the flaming arrows of temptation, doubt, blasphemy, and despair; the fiery darts; the temptation; the doubt; the blasphemy; the despair. The expositors note the fall harmless: caught on the shield of faith, the darts fall harmless and burnt out; the fall harmless; the burnt out; the caught; the quenched; the the shield. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the above all taking the shield of faith, the quench all the fiery darts of the wicked, and the shield of faith. The verse thus records the shield of faith — teaching that faith, above the rest, catches and quenches Satan's fiery darts.
17:
"And take the helmet of salvation, and the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God:" The witnesses note the helmet of salvation and the sword of the Spirit. Gill notes the helmet of salvation may mean Christ himself the Saviour, or the salvation he is the author of, and a well-grounded hope of it, which defends the head against false doctrines as a helmet guards the head. The witnesses note Paul directing them to take the helmet of salvation and the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God; the helmet of hope and the offensive sword of Scripture — that Paul names the last two pieces: the "helmet of salvation" (the assured hope of salvation in Christ, which lifts up the head and guards the mind against discouragement and error), and, the one weapon of attack, "the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God" — the Scripture wielded in the Spirit's power, the keen blade by which the believer both wards off and strikes down the enemy, as Christ himself repelled the tempter with "it is written"; the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God. The witnesses note the helmet of salvation. The witnesses note the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God.

The witnesses note the helmet of salvation and the sword of the Spirit — the assured hope guarding the head, and the word of God the one weapon of attack.

So the witnesses make the verse the helmet of hope and the sword of Scripture. The lesson the expositors draw is that the hope of salvation guards the mind, and the word of God is the believer's one weapon of attack. The witnesses note the helmet: "take the helmet of salvation" — Gill notes the helmet is Christ the Saviour, or the hope of salvation; the helmet; the salvation; the Christ; the hope; the guard. The expositors observe the guarding the head: the helmet guards the head (the mind) against discouragement and error; the guarding the head; the the mind; the discouragement; the error; the lifts up. The witnesses press the sword: "the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God" — the sword of the Spirit, the word of God; the sword; the of the Spirit; the the word of God; the Scripture; the blade. The expositors draw the lesson on the offensive weapon: the sword is the one weapon of attack — Scripture wielded in the Spirit's power; the offensive weapon; the the sword; the attack; the Scripture; the the Spirit's power. The witnesses observe the wards off and strikes: by the sword the believer both wards off and strikes down the enemy; the wards off; the strikes down; the the enemy; the both; the blade. The expositors note Christ's example: as Christ repelled the tempter with "it is written"; the Christ's example; the the tempter; the it is written; the repelled; the Scripture. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the helmet of salvation, the the sword of the Spirit which is the word of God, and the helmet and the sword. The verse thus records the helmet of hope and the sword of Scripture — teaching that the hope of salvation guards the mind, and the word of God is the believer's one weapon of attack.
18:
"Praying always with all prayer and supplication in the Spirit, and watching thereunto with all perseverance and supplication for all saints;" The witnesses note prayer as the atmosphere of the whole conflict. Gill notes the last weapon is prayer, taking in all sorts — mental and vocal, public and private — and every branch of it, deprecation, petition, and thanksgiving, used always, opposed to those who pray not at all or only in distress. The witnesses note Paul calling them to pray always with all prayer and supplication in the Spirit, watching thereunto with perseverance and supplication for all saints; the all-pervading practice of watchful, persevering prayer — that Paul, having named the armour, adds the spirit in which it is all to be worn and the warfare waged: constant, varied prayer ("all prayer and supplication"), offered "in the Spirit" (by his help and influence), kept up with alert watchfulness and unwearied perseverance, and reaching out in intercession not for self alone but for all the saints, the whole army together; praying always... with all perseverance. The witnesses note praying always with all prayer and supplication in the Spirit. The witnesses note watching thereunto with all perseverance and supplication for all saints.

The witnesses note prayer as the atmosphere of the conflict — constant, varied prayer in the Spirit, watchful and persevering, interceding for all the saints.

So the witnesses make the verse the all-pervading practice of prayer. The lesson the expositors draw is that the whole warfare is to be waged in constant, watchful, persevering prayer in the Spirit, for all the saints. The witnesses note the praying always: "praying always with all prayer and supplication" — Gill notes prayer of all sorts, used always; the praying always; the all prayer; the supplication; the all sorts; the constant. The expositors observe the in the Spirit: "in the Spirit" — offered in the Spirit (by his help and influence); the in the Spirit; the his help; the his influence; the empowered; the prayer. The witnesses press the watching: "watching thereunto with all perseverance" — watching with alert watchfulness and unwearied perseverance; the watching; the perseverance; the alert; the unwearied; the kept up. The expositors draw the lesson on the atmosphere of conflict: prayer is the spirit in which the armour is worn and the warfare waged; the atmosphere; the the spirit; the the armour; the the warfare; the waged. The witnesses observe the for all saints: "and supplication for all saints" — interceding not for self alone but for all the saints; the for all saints; the intercession; the not self alone; the the whole army; the together. The expositors note the watchful and persevering: prayer is to be watchful and unwearied; the watchful; the persevering; the unwearied; the alert; the constant. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the praying always with all prayer and supplication in the Spirit, the watching thereunto with all perseverance and supplication for all saints, and the prayer. The verse thus records the all-pervading practice of prayer — teaching that the whole warfare is to be waged in constant, watchful, persevering prayer in the Spirit, for all the saints.
19:
"And for me, that utterance may be given unto me, that I may open my mouth boldly, to make known the mystery of the gospel," The witnesses note Paul's request for prayer for his bold preaching. Gill notes this shows the apostle's great humility and his sense of the greatness of the work of the ministry, that it is the duty of people to pray for their ministers, and that the more superior members need the help of the inferior. The witnesses note Paul asking that they pray for him, that utterance be given him to open his mouth boldly and make known the mystery of the gospel; the apostle's humble plea for utterance and boldness — that Paul, who has just commanded prayer for all the saints, asks a place in it for himself, requesting not relief from his bonds but two gifts for his ministry: "utterance" (the right words, given of God) and "boldness" (freedom and courage to speak), that even in prison he might make known the gospel-mystery without fear; that utterance may be given unto me. The witnesses note for me, that utterance may be given unto me. The witnesses note to make known the mystery of the gospel.

The witnesses note Paul's humble request for prayer — that utterance and boldness be given him to make known the mystery of the gospel, even in his bonds.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's plea for utterance and boldness. The lesson the expositors draw is the apostle's humble request for the gifts of speech and courage for his ministry. The witnesses note the for me: "and for me" — Gill notes Paul, in humility, asks prayer for himself; the for me; the humility; the asks prayer; the for himself; the a place. The expositors observe the utterance: "that utterance may be given unto me" — that utterance (the right words) be given him; the utterance; the the right words; the given; the of God; the speech. The witnesses press the boldness: "that I may open my mouth boldly" — that he may speak with boldness (freedom and courage); the boldness; the freedom; the courage; the open my mouth; the speak. The expositors draw the lesson on the not relief but ministry: Paul asks not relief from his bonds, but gifts for his ministry; the not relief; the not the bonds; the for his ministry; the the gifts; the the work. The witnesses observe the mystery of the gospel: "to make known the mystery of the gospel" — to make known the gospel-mystery; the mystery of the gospel; the make known; the the gospel; the the secret; the proclaim. The expositors note the even in prison: even in prison Paul would make known the gospel without fear; the even in prison; the the gospel; the without fear; the the bonds; the proclaim. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for me that utterance may be given unto me, the to make known the mystery of the gospel, and the plea for utterance. The verse thus records Paul's plea for utterance and boldness — teaching the apostle's humble request for the gifts of speech and courage for his ministry.
20:
"For which I am an ambassador in bonds: that therein I may speak boldly, as I ought to speak." The witnesses note Paul as an ambassador in chains. Gill notes Paul bore the character of an ambassador — from and for Christ, in his room and stead, representing him, sent by him, entrusted with the gospel and a commission to preach it, and furnished with qualifications for it. The witnesses note Paul describing himself as an ambassador in bonds, that he might speak boldly as he ought; the strange dignity of an ambassador in chains — that Paul names his high office (ambassador of Christ, the King's representative bearing terms of peace) joined to the strange indignity of his condition (in bonds, a chained envoy, contrary to all the honour due an ambassador), and prays that even so he may have grace to speak with the boldness his great commission demands, as becomes one who bears the message of the King of heaven; an ambassador in bonds. The witnesses note for which I am an ambassador in bonds. The witnesses note that therein I may speak boldly, as I ought to speak.

The witnesses note Paul "an ambassador in bonds" — the King's envoy in chains — praying for grace to speak with the boldness his great commission demands.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul the ambassador in chains. The lesson the expositors draw is the strange dignity of the King's envoy in bonds, praying for boldness becoming his commission. The witnesses note the ambassador: "for which I am an ambassador" — Gill notes Paul's office as ambassador of Christ; the ambassador; the of Christ; the the King's envoy; the representative; the office. The expositors observe the in bonds: "in bonds" — an ambassador in bonds (a chained envoy); the in bonds; the chained; the the chains; the the indignity; the prison. The witnesses press the strange dignity: the high office joined to the strange indignity of chains; the strange dignity; the the office; the the indignity; the contrary; the the honour. The expositors draw the lesson on the envoy in chains: an ambassador in bonds is contrary to all the honour due an envoy; the envoy in chains; the the honour; the contrary; the the indignity; the strange. The witnesses observe the speak boldly: "that therein I may speak boldly, as I ought to speak" — that he may speak with the boldness his commission demands; the speak boldly; the as I ought; the the boldness; the the commission; the demands. The expositors note the King's message: Paul bears the message of the King of heaven; the King's message; the the King; the of heaven; the the message; the bears. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the for which I am an ambassador in bonds, the that therein I may speak boldly as I ought to speak, and the ambassador in chains. The verse thus records Paul the ambassador in chains — teaching the strange dignity of the King's envoy in bonds, praying for boldness becoming his commission.
21:
"But that ye also may know my affairs, and how I do, Tychicus, a beloved brother and faithful minister in the Lord, shall make known to you all things:" The witnesses note the sending of Tychicus with news of Paul. Gill notes Paul would have them know his affairs, both temporal and spiritual — that he was in bonds, how he was supported, how he preached in his own hired house and with what success, and the state of things at Rome. The witnesses note Paul declaring that Tychicus, a beloved brother and faithful minister in the Lord, would make known to them all his affairs and how he fared; the trusted messenger sent with tidings — that Paul, knowing the Ephesians' loving concern for him, sends Tychicus (whom he commends as a beloved brother and faithful minister) to bring them word of all his circumstances and welfare, that they might be relieved of anxiety and informed of how things stood with the imprisoned apostle; Tychicus... shall make known to you all things. The witnesses note that ye also may know my affairs, and how I do. The witnesses note Tychicus, a beloved brother and faithful minister in the Lord.

The witnesses note the sending of Tychicus — a beloved brother and faithful minister — to bring the Ephesians word of Paul's affairs and how he fared.

So the witnesses make the verse the sending of Tychicus with tidings. The lesson the expositors draw is the trusted messenger sent to inform the loving church of Paul's circumstances. The witnesses note the affairs: "that ye also may know my affairs, and how I do" — Gill notes Paul would have them know his affairs; the affairs; the my affairs; the how I do; the welfare; the circumstances. The expositors observe the Tychicus: "Tychicus, a beloved brother and faithful minister in the Lord" — Tychicus, the beloved brother and faithful minister; the Tychicus; the beloved brother; the faithful minister; the in the Lord; the commended. The witnesses press the make known: "shall make known to you all things" — Tychicus shall make known all things; the make known; the all things; the report; the tidings; the inform. The expositors draw the lesson on the trusted messenger: Paul sends a trusted messenger to bring word; the trusted messenger; the sent; the bring word; the the tidings; the inform. The witnesses observe the loving concern: Paul knows the Ephesians' loving concern for him; the loving concern; the the Ephesians; the for him; the anxious; the care. The expositors note the relieved of anxiety: the news would relieve them of anxiety; the relieved; the anxiety; the the news; the informed; the comforted. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the that ye also may know my affairs and how I do, the Tychicus a beloved brother and faithful minister, and the sending of Tychicus. The verse thus records the sending of Tychicus with tidings — teaching the trusted messenger sent to inform the loving church of Paul's circumstances.
22:
"Whom I have sent unto you for the same purpose, that ye might know our affairs, and that he might comfort your hearts." The witnesses note the double purpose of sending Tychicus. Gill notes this shows the great concern the apostle had for the churches, being willing to inform them of everything that might be for their use and service. Barnes notes the churches felt a great interest in Paul's welfare, he being a prisoner with an uncertain outcome. The witnesses note Paul stating that he sent Tychicus for this very purpose — that they might know his affairs and that he might comfort their hearts; the messenger sent to inform and to comfort — that Paul names the twofold aim of the mission: not only that the Ephesians might be informed of his circumstances, but that Tychicus, by his presence and report, might comfort and encourage their hearts (perhaps cast down by anxiety over their imprisoned apostle), so that the errand was an act of pastoral love, caring for the comfort of the church even from his prison; that he might comfort your hearts. The witnesses note that ye might know our affairs. The witnesses note that he might comfort your hearts.

The witnesses note the double purpose of sending Tychicus — to inform the Ephesians of Paul's affairs and to comfort their hearts; an act of pastoral love from prison.

So the witnesses make the verse the double purpose of the mission. The lesson the expositors draw is that Tychicus was sent both to inform the church and to comfort their hearts. The witnesses note the same purpose: "whom I have sent unto you for the same purpose" — Paul sent Tychicus for this very purpose; the same purpose; the sent; the Tychicus; the the mission; the aim. The expositors observe the know our affairs: "that ye might know our affairs" — that they might know his affairs; the know our affairs; the informed; the circumstances; the welfare; the report. The witnesses press the comfort: "that he might comfort your hearts" — that he might comfort their hearts; the comfort; the their hearts; the encourage; the cheer; the console. The expositors draw the lesson on the twofold aim: the mission's aim was both to inform and to comfort; the twofold aim; the inform; the comfort; the both; the mission. The witnesses observe the cast down by anxiety: the Ephesians were perhaps cast down by anxiety over their imprisoned apostle; the cast down; the anxiety; the their apostle; the imprisoned; the concern. The expositors note the pastoral love: the errand was an act of pastoral love, even from prison; the pastoral love; the the errand; the from prison; the caring; the the church. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the that ye might know our affairs, the that he might comfort your hearts, and the double purpose. The verse thus records the double purpose of the mission — teaching that Tychicus was sent both to inform the church and to comfort their hearts.
23:
"Peace be to the brethren, and love with faith, from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ." The witnesses note the closing benediction of peace, love, and faith. Gill notes "peace" means all prosperity outward and inward, temporal, spiritual, and eternal — especially peace of conscience under the sprinkling of Christ's blood and a view of peace made with God by it. The witnesses note Paul closing with a benediction of peace to the brethren, and love with faith, from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ; the apostolic blessing of peace, love, and faith — that Paul, drawing toward the end, invokes on the brethren the great gospel blessings: "peace" (reconciliation with God and the calm of conscience that flows from it), and "love with faith" (a love joined to and springing from faith), and names their source — God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ together, the one fountain of all grace; peace be to the brethren, and love with faith. The witnesses note peace be to the brethren, and love with faith. The witnesses note from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.

The witnesses note the closing benediction — peace, and love with faith, from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ — the great gospel blessings invoked on the brethren.

So the witnesses make the verse the benediction of peace, love, and faith. The lesson the expositors draw is the invoking of the great gospel blessings from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ. The witnesses note the peace: "peace be to the brethren" — Gill notes peace, all prosperity, especially peace of conscience; the peace; the the brethren; the reconciliation; the conscience; the prosperity. The expositors observe the love with faith: "and love with faith" — love joined to and springing from faith; the love with faith; the love; the faith; the joined; the springing. The witnesses press the source: "from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ" — from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ; the source; the the Father; the the Lord Jesus; the together; the the fountain. The expositors draw the lesson on the gospel blessings: Paul invokes the great gospel blessings — peace, love, faith; the gospel blessings; the peace; the love; the faith; the invoked. The witnesses observe the one fountain: the Father and the Son together are the one fountain of all grace; the one fountain; the the Father and Son; the together; the all grace; the source. The expositors note the apostolic blessing: the closing apostolic blessing on the brethren; the apostolic blessing; the closing; the the brethren; the benediction; the invoked. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the peace be to the brethren and love with faith, the from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ, and the benediction. The verse thus records the benediction of peace, love, and faith — teaching the invoking of the great gospel blessings from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ.
24:
"Grace be with all them that love our Lord Jesus Christ in sincerity. Amen." The witnesses note the final benediction of grace on all who sincerely love Christ. Gill notes Christ is the object of love, a lovely object — to be loved because of the loveliness of his person, his suitableness and fulness as a Saviour, his great love shown to his people, and the relations he stands in to them. The witnesses note Paul ending the epistle with a benediction of grace on all who love the Lord Jesus Christ in sincerity, sealed with "Amen"; the closing grace on all sincere lovers of Christ — that Paul, with his last words, invokes the grace of God on a marked company: all who love the Lord Jesus Christ "in sincerity" (with a true, incorruptible, unfeigned love, not in word or pretence only), a love that is itself the mark of the true believer, so that the apostle's parting blessing rests on every soul, of whatever place or rank, that truly and sincerely loves the Saviour; grace be with all them that love our Lord Jesus Christ in sincerity. The witnesses note grace be with all them that love our Lord Jesus Christ. The witnesses note in sincerity. Amen.

The witnesses note the final benediction — grace on all who love the Lord Jesus Christ in sincerity — a true, unfeigned love the mark of the believer, sealed with "Amen."

So the witnesses make the verse the closing grace on all who sincerely love Christ. The lesson the expositors draw is the parting blessing on every soul that truly and sincerely loves the Saviour. The witnesses note the grace: "grace be with all them" — Paul invokes grace on all; the grace; the be with all; the invoked; the blessing; the all. The expositors observe the love Christ: "that love our Lord Jesus Christ" — all who love the Lord Jesus Christ; the love Christ; the the Lord Jesus; the loving; the the Saviour; the marked. The witnesses press the in sincerity: "in sincerity" — with a true, incorruptible, unfeigned love; the in sincerity; the true; the unfeigned; the incorruptible; the not pretence. The expositors draw the lesson on the mark of the believer: sincere love to Christ is the mark of the true believer; the mark; the sincere love; the the believer; the true; the test. The witnesses observe the every soul: the blessing rests on every soul, of whatever place or rank, that sincerely loves Christ; the every soul; the whatever place; the rank; the sincerely; the all. The expositors note the Amen: the epistle is sealed with "Amen"; the Amen; the sealed; the so be it; the the close; the the epistle. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the grace be with all them that love our Lord Jesus Christ, the in sincerity Amen, and the closing grace. The verse thus records the closing grace on all who sincerely love Christ — teaching the parting blessing on every soul that truly and sincerely loves the Saviour.
